results.possibleIdentity: Adrianne Wortzel (born October 7, 1941) is a pioneering American media artist and educator based in New York City whose career spans over six decades. During the late 1960s and 1970s, her practice primarily focused on abstract painting and sculpture. She studied at the Brooklyn Museum Art School (1956–1963) and Brooklyn College, where her mentors included Ad Reinhardt, Louise Bourgeois, and Jimmy Ernst. In this early period, she developed a unique visual language using spattered acrylic paint and created works such as motorized geometric sculptures and paintings that simulated faux-text manuscripts. Her work was exhibited internationally during the 1970s, including solo exhibitions in Sweden, leading to the acquisition of her pieces by the Moderna Museet in Stockholm. In the 1990s, Wortzel pivoted to new media and telerobotics, earning an MFA in Computer Arts from the School of Visual Arts in 1996. She is widely recognized for integrating robotics into performance art and installations, such as 'Camouflage Town' (2001) at the Whitney Museum of American Art. Her work often explores the intersection of human experience and technological evolution, themes historically associated with organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Wortzel is a Professor Emerita at the New York City College of Technology (CUNY) and has received numerous honors, including grants from the National Science Foundation and the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation.

results.possibleIdentity: Harley Jones is a filmmaker, film editor, and educator who was a central figure in the development of experimental and documentary film in Wales during the late 1960s and 1970s. He began his career as the youngest film editor at the BBC Film Unit (at age 18) and served as an editorial assistant to the documentary pioneer John Grierson on the program 'This Wonderful World' from 1963 to 1966. In 1966, he co-founded the Newport Film Unit at the Newport College of Art and Design alongside Principal John Wright, serving as its first course leader until 1982. Under his leadership, the unit became a hub for modernist and experimental film, producing works that blended documentary style with animation and 'artists' moving image' techniques. His own films, such as 'After Many a Summer' (1968), 'Promenade' (1969), and 'Resurrection', are noted for their impressionistic and documentary qualities; 'After Many a Summer' was acquired by the Contemporary Art Society for Wales. Jones was also an active member of the Arts Council of Great Britain's Film Committee in the 1970s, where he influenced policy regarding film and video as art forms. His association with cross-disciplinary experiments is highlighted by his teaching at Newport, which fostered collaborations between filmmakers, photographers (such as David Hurn), and conceptual artists (such as Keith Arnatt), paralleling the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: John Nagle was an electrical engineer and technical collaborator associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Working at Bell Laboratories (Bell Labs) in Holmdel, New Jersey, he was a member of the engineering teams that partnered with prominent artists such as Robert Whitman and Robert Rauschenberg. Nagle is recognized for his contributions to the technical design and execution of the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, a landmark project of E.A.T. that required large-scale collaboration between artists and engineers. His role within E.A.T. exemplified the organization's mission to integrate new technology into the creative arts, often involving specialized work in electronics and early computer-aided systems. Additionally, a John Nagle (likely the same individual or a close contemporary in the same professional circle) is noted in historical archives as a pioneer electrical engineer and ham radio enthusiast (callsign K4KJ) who also worked as a transmitter engineer for NBC.

John Nagle (also known as John F. Nagle) is a physicist and researcher active in the 1960s and 1970s who demonstrated a crossover between scientific research and artistic representation. While primarily known for his work in nuclear physics and biological sciences at institutions like MIT and Carnegie Mellon University, he was involved in projects where 'the poet and artist' within the scientist were encouraged to emerge. Notably, during the late 1960s at MIT's Laboratory for Nuclear Science (LNS), he was tasked with using early computer technology to 'embellish' and illustrate the complex developmental lineages of particle physics. This type of interdisciplinary computer-aided visualization aligned with the contemporaneous interests of organizations like E.A.T. and MIT’s Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS), which sought to merge technological expertise with artistic expression.
